title: Swapfile on /realm blocks btrbk snapshots — move to dedicated subvolume
description: The 2026-07-09 swapfile move to /realm (hosts/sinnix-prime/storage.nix) creates /realm/swap with mkdir -p, i.e. a plain directory inside the /realm subvolume. An active swapfile pins its extents, so 'btrfs subvolume snapshot /realm' fails with ETXTBSY ('Text file busy'). Evidence: swapfile created 22:33, btrbk last success 22:30, first failure 23:00, failing every 30min since; borg realm archive 11.4h stale vs 6h SLO; borgbackup-status red hourly. Fix: prepare-swapfile must create /realm/swap as a btrfs SUBVOLUME (nested subvols are excluded from parent snapshots by design) and migrate the existing plain-dir layout. Also apply the same live migration immediately to unblock the backup pipeline without waiting for a switch.
notes: Fixed in two commits + live migration, all verified 2026-07-10: (1) Live migration 11:50: swapoff (file was 0B used) -> rm -> rmdir -> btrfs subvolume create /realm/swap -> chattr +C -> recreate 8G swapfile -> swapon. btrbk green at 11:52:01 (snapshot realm.20260710T115159 created, first success since 22:30 yesterday); borgbackup-job-realm drain started 11:52:16. (2) Commit 4721850d: prepare-swapfile creates the swap dir as a btrfs subvolume and migrates plain-dir layouts (swapoff only if active via swapon-show grep, rmdir refuses non-empty). (3) BONUS latent bug found during verification, commit 54fbab32: the unit wiring (prepare requiredBy/before, drain after) still referenced swap-swapfile.swap — the OLD /swap/swapfile path's escaped unit name; the real unit is realm-swap-swapfile.swap. systemd listed the old name as not-found, so on fresh boot the swap unit would activate without prepare. Fixed by deriving via utils.escapeSystemdPath from swapFile so it can never drift again. Note sinnix-ethereal's hardcoded swap-swapfile.swap is currently CORRECT for its /swap/swapfile path; same derive pattern could be applied there for hygiene but was out of scope. Verification: two switches applied (live rev 54fbab32); RequiredBy/Before=realm-swap-swapfile.swap live; unit restart applied declared priority 10; prepare-swapfile re-run no-ops with active swapfile (subvol inode 256 preserved); btrbk manual run green; borg drain in flight at close time — borgbackup-status flips green on next hourly run after drain completes.
close_reason: Root-caused (swapfile-in-parent-subvolume blocks btrfs snapshots, ETXTBSY) and fixed: live migration to dedicated subvolume restored btrbk at 11:52; config now creates/migrates the subvolume declaratively; latent unit-name drift (swap-swapfile.swap vs realm-swap-swapfile.swap) also found and fixed via utils.escapeSystemdPath. Both switches applied and verified live.

title: Host-wide heavy-work lease with attested ownership
description: Multiple source-only agents should run concurrently, but build, test, archive-rebuild, Nix, and other contended phases must not overlap accidentally. Prompt-only coordination failed on 2026-07-10 when a Sinex worker ran xtask tests/checks while the Polylogue index rebuild held the declared heavy permit. Add one host-level execution lease; do not create a task queue, scheduler, or workflow DSL.
design: Extend the existing Sinnix execution/resource wrappers with a narrow heavy-work lease. A launch declares job id, repo/worktree, command class, cgroup/process, and artifact refs; acquisition is atomic and records the live holder under XDG runtime/state. Nested wrappers inherit one lease. A second heavy launch fails closed or returns a truthful bounded wait/refusal receipt before work starts, while source reading/editing/model reasoning remains unconstrained. Stale-holder recovery requires PID+cgroup/start-time attestation, never PID alone. The lease must cover cross-repo wrappers and explicit archive/Nix maintenance, including coordinator-launched jobs.
acceptance_criteria: Two disjoint source-only jobs can remain active while exactly one enters a synthetic heavy phase. A concurrent second heavy phase does not execute and reports the attested holder plus retry/refusal state. Completion, interruption, and crash release or safely recover the lease; PID reuse/cgroup mismatch cannot steal it. Nested xtask/cargo/nix wrappers do not deadlock or double-acquire. A real cross-repo canary sequences one Polylogue and one Sinex focused check and preserves receipts for both. No Beads authority, prioritization, or durable task queue is added.

title: Attested headless agent job handles and inner-scope resource controls
description: First execution slice: replace ad-hoc systemd-run plus PID/title inference with one reusable headless-agent job handle. The handle must bind the declared job to its repo/worktree, backend/model/effort, prompt and result files, launcher unit, actual sinnix-agent scope, process identity, and lifecycle. Resource options must reach the inner Sinnix scope that owns Codex/Claude, not merely an almost-empty outer service.
design: Extend existing dots/_ai/skills/agent-orchestration scripts and scripts/sinnix-agent-scope-exec; do not add another task scheduler. run_agent_prompt emits an atomic JSON manifest under XDG state and updates it across starting/running/completed/failed. Discover the child scope through explicit launch attestation rather than process-table guessing. Add validated per-job MemoryHigh/MemoryMax and CPU/IO weight overrides to the inner scope. A status/list command reads manifests plus live systemd state. Cancel/interrupt accepts an attested job ID and refuses ambiguous PID/window/title targeting. Keep Kitty control as an optional viewport, not identity.
acceptance_criteria: A fake/lightweight backend test launches two distinct jobs and produces manifests binding job id, repo/worktree, backend/model, prompt hash, output paths, launcher unit, actual inner scope and PID. Status reports live state and effective inner-scope MemoryHigh/MemoryMax. A negative test proves cancel refuses a PID/title/window-only or mismatched handle. Completion persists exit status and artifact refs. Existing run_agent_prompt/launch_agent_tabs callers remain usable. Shell checks and focused tests pass; no Nix rebuild is required for the test.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text: Scope (2026-07-10): implement one attested, headless local-agent job handle by extending run_agent_prompt.sh and sinnix-agent-scope-exec. This slice owns atomic manifest lifecycle, scope/PID/worktree attestation, narrow resource overrides, status/list/interrupt by job ID, and lightweight fake-backend tests. It preserves direct callers. Excluded: Beads task authority, queues/schedulers/conductor DSL, Kitty/window identity, heavy-work permits, Nix evaluation/build/switch, and real-model tests.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text: Implemented in a4fdef4 on feature/feat/attested-agent-jobs. The runner now writes atomic XDG-state job manifests through starting/running/completed/failed, creates one named inner agent scope with validated MemoryHigh/MemoryMax/CPUWeight/IOWeight overrides, and records process/cgroup/worktree/prompt/artifact bindings. agent_job_control.sh refreshes live systemd facts and interrupts only after PID, cgroup, scope, and cwd/worktree attestation; PID/title selectors are rejected. The focused fake-backend test covers two jobs, completion artifacts, effective limits, and mismatch rejection. Verification: bash -n, focused test, git diff --check. Deferred by host constraint: Nix evaluation/build/switch and real-model execution.

title: Agent execution control plane: attested handles, bounded resources, sequenced heavy work
description: Centralize local frontier-agent execution in Sinnix so multiple headless Codex/Claude lanes can be launched, observed, limited, and integrated without terminal inference. This is execution substrate only: Beads remains task authority and Polylogue remains evidence/history; this program must not grow a second task queue or conductor vocabulary.
design: Extend the existing agent-orchestration skill, run_agent_prompt.sh, and sinnix-agent-scope-exec. Deliver an attested job handle binding logical agent/session, process and actual inner cgroup, repo/worktree/branch, prompt/output artifacts, model/effort, resource policy, lifecycle, and receipts. Add a host-wide heavy-work permit that queues only build/test/rebuild phases while source-only reasoning lanes may continue. Backends are adapters for Codex exec, Claude print/web where viable, and Codex Cloud task handles. Coordination consumers may project these facts, but do not own scheduling authority.
acceptance_criteria: From one command the operator can launch at least two source-only headless jobs in separate worktrees, list truthful state and actual cgroup memory for each, and release their heavy verification phases serially. Destructive control refuses a PID/window-only target without positive handle attestation. Job completion preserves prompt hash, logs, final output, diff/commit refs, verification receipt, and resource summary. Beads remains the only durable task state; no second queue/state machine is introduced.

title: Harden local and cloud agent orchestration controls
description: The shared agent-orchestration skill cannot reliably launch Claude on this host: the managed binary is claude-full, the runner passes an unsupported --workdir flag, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is ambient and can accidentally bypass subscription auth, Claude model/effort are ignored, batch prompts are serialized, and Kitty launches can take focus. Codex Cloud CLI and Claude native background controls are also undocumented despite being the most reliable hosted/local adapters.
design: Update dots/_ai/skills/agent-orchestration scripts and references plus the shared desktop-control skill and global CLAUDE.md. Resolve managed agent binaries, default Claude runs to env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY with an explicit API-key opt-in, pass model/effort/schema correctly, persist Claude last output, add bounded --parallel batch execution, and make Kitty launches keep focus with optional silent workspace routing. Document codex cloud exec/list/status/diff/apply, Claude --background plus agents/logs/stop, background CDP tab ownership, and cloud uv-run discipline. Preserve existing dirty user changes by staging only new hunks.
acceptance_criteria: bash -n passes for changed shell scripts; dry-runs prove explicit Sonnet and Codex model commands, subscription-auth unsetting, bounded parallel mode, and Kitty --keep-focus/workspace routing; a live minimal Claude subscription probe and native background lifecycle complete; canonical agent-topology verification passes; only task-owned hunks/files are committed and pushed on Sinnix master.
close_reason: Implemented managed Claude/Codex runtime selection, subscription-safe Claude auth, explicit model/effort/schema output handling, bounded parallel batch execution, keep-focus Kitty routing, Codex Cloud/native Claude documentation, and focus-safe browser background-tab/file-upload controls. Verified bash -n, ShellCheck, two skill validators, explicit Claude/Codex/Kitty dry runs, invalid-option guards, a live Sonnet run with an intentionally invalid ambient API key (subscription fallback returned probe-a), background CDP creation/upload with OS focus unchanged, and git diff checks. verify-agent-topology reports Sinnix/Polylogue/Sinex/Lynchpin OK but retains 14 pre-existing inactive/archive/legacy repo failures; no claim of a fleet-wide pass.

title: polylogued reads ~1.3 TiB/day from disk — likely whole-file re-parse of session JSONL on every append
description: process_io_delta_sample for 2026-07-09 attributes 1322 GiB of block reads to polylogued.service in one day — the single largest reader on the host, ~3x machine-telemetry, and a constant page-cache evictor that amplifies memory pressure (workingset refaults) for everything else. Hypothesis: the ingestion daemon re-reads/re-parses entire session JSONL files (some sessions exceed 13k messages) on every inotify append event instead of tailing incrementally from a persisted offset. Needs confirmation in the polylogue repo (fix belongs there; this bead tracks the host-health finding and evidence). Evidence query: SELECT comm, unit, SUM(read_bytes_delta) FROM process_io_delta_sample WHERE observed_at >= '2026-07-09' GROUP BY comm, unit — polylogued 1322 GiB vs machine-telemetry 416 GiB vs postgres 166 GiB.
notes: MECHANISM IDENTIFIED (2026-07-10 01:45, live daemon 4124822): NOT a read-amplification bug — live.watcher append-mode reads are excellent (read=0.2MB for 62MB of changed sessions, read_amp 0.004x), so the 1.3TiB/day attribution was dominated by the now-removed cache-trim evictions. The RSS balloon is the convergence cycle: every ~90s the watcher catch-up ingests the changed live sessions and runs the FULL pipeline in-process — parse (7.4s), embed (24.4s!), insights, graph_resolve, thread_refresh — allocating a multi-GB transient working set each cycle; CPython retains the peak as fragmented heap, so RSS ratchets to the cgroup ceiling (unit already has MemoryHigh=4G/MemoryMax=6G mkForce'd in sinnix — it is guarded, just chronically pinned at throttle). Fix direction in polylogue repo: (a) bound the per-cycle working set (stream parse, chunked embed), (b) run embed/convergence in a short-lived subprocess so memory is returned to the OS per cycle, (c) reconsider embedding on every 90s tick for still-growing live sessions — debounce until session quiesces. Daemon heartbeat: 17087 sessions, 4.45M messages indexed.

title: sinex cachix substitution contract is dead: no CI workflow pushes sinexd package since sinex#883
description: CLAUDE.md documents that sinex CI pushes package builds to sinity.cachix.org so sinnix-prime substitutes instead of compiling the ~464K-LOC workspace. sinex commit 013c2b11d 'ci: disable automatic paid Actions (#883)' made every workflow workflow_dispatch-only except verify-perf (which only runs perf contracts and is currently failing). Result: every sinex input bump in sinnix (e.g. 85173007 bumping to 86d2fe40) forces a local nix-sandbox build where the single sinexd rustc invocation peaks at 8-11.5GB RSS; on 2026-07-09 earlyoom killed rustc 17 times and the switch never activated (live drift: running d4079514 vs HEAD c47a451b). Fix direction: either restore a master-push cachix package-build workflow in sinex (scoped to the package derivation, not full CI), or push the locally-built sinexd store path to cachix from the desktop after each successful build, or update the CLAUDE.md contract to stop promising substitution. Also update stale CLAUDE.md text either way.
close_reason: Contract redefined + implemented: desktop is now builder of record. sinexCachePush (flake/command-registry.nix, spliced into both switch paths) publishes the sinex closure to sinity.cachix.org as a detached background.slice user unit after successful activation. Verified live 2026-07-10 00:33: pushed sinex-0.4.2 (403 MiB). Stale CI-push claims corrected in flake.nix comment and CLAUDE.md. Upstream memory-peak/CI options filed as Sinity/sinex#2456.

title: Devshell pytest temp trees accumulate in tmpfs /tmp and pin gigabytes of RAM
description: Each nix develop session gets a fresh /tmp/nix-shell.XXXXXX TMPDIR on the 6G tmpfs. Lynchpin/sinex pytest runs write large fixture trees (duckdb substrate files ~21MB per test) into pytest-of-sinity under that TMPDIR. pytest's keep-3-runs retention never fires across sessions because every shell has a distinct basetemp root, so trees accumulate forever: on 2026-07-10, 345 stale nix-shell dirs held 5.8G of 6G tmpfs = 5.8G of RAM pinned as shmem, directly causing earlyoom storms. Fix direction: point devshell TMPDIR (or pytest basetemp via addopts -p basetemp) at /realm/tmp for heavy repos, and/or add a systemd-tmpfiles age rule for /tmp/nix-shell.* older than ~2 days. Evidence: kill_event table 2026-07-09 (17 rustc kills), /tmp at 96% with zero live owners of the big trees.
close_reason: Fixed in workstation profile: environment.sessionVariables.TMPDIR=/realm/tmp/shell moves all devshell/agent scratch (incl. nix develop's nix-shell.XXXXXX trees and pytest fixtures) off the 6G /tmp tmpfs onto NVMe, with a tmpfiles 'd /realm/tmp/shell 1777 root root 7d' aging rule bounding accumulation. Verified live 2026-07-10 00:33: /etc/set-environment exports it, dir exists. /tmp keeps its tmpfs role + upstream 10d aging for husks. Immediate debris (5.8G) was cleaned manually earlier the same night.

title: Fix Codex npm self-update prefix
description: Codex update invokes npm from the npm-installed CLI. The generated launcher only prepends the per-tool npm bin dir, so npm resolves its global prefix to the immutable Nix nodejs store and fails creating lib. Persist the mutable npm prefix into the generated launcher and cover it with the agent wrapper runtime test.
close_reason: Fixed by exporting the per-tool npm prefix from generated agent launchers. Verified with bash -n scripts/sinnix-agent-npm-bootstrap, a throwaway HOME bootstrap harness proving npm_config_prefix and NPM_CONFIG_PREFIX are inherited by the launched Codex binary, nix develop --command lint, and env npm_config_prefix=$HOME/.local/state/codex/npm NPM_CONFIG_PREFIX=$HOME/.local/state/codex/npm codex update.

title: switch pipeline silently fails to register the boot profile when any unit fails during activation
description:
Discovered 2026-07-08 while doing pre-reboot due diligence: /nix/var/nix/profiles/system was stuck at generation 453 (built 2026-07-04 18:12) despite many subsequent successful-looking 'switch' runs across multiple sessions (including the sinnix-tco Step 1 batch on 2026-07-06 and tonight's batch fix pass) -- confirmed via 'nh os info' which reported 'Profile is out of sync with /run/current-system. This may happen if a previous switch failed during activation.' /run/current-system was correct/current the whole time; only the persistent boot-default profile and bootloader entry were stale. A reboot during this window would have silently reverted 4+ days of config work.

Root cause: nh os switch's internal activation step treats ANY failed systemd unit (here: nvidia-container-toolkit-cdi-generator.service, which has been failing with a driver/library version mismatch since around 2026-07-04 and can only be fixed by a reboot -- a known, expected, pre-existing failure, not a config regression) as a hard activation failure (nh error: 'Activation (test) failed', exit status 4 from switch-to-configuration). This triggers this repo's own fallback in flake/command-registry.nix's appCommands.switch / flake/dev-shell.nix's mkNhCommand (the 'trying exact toplevel activation fallback' path, which calls switch-to-configuration switch directly via sudo, bypassing nh) -- but that fallback's own switch-to-configuration invocation ALSO fails on the same unit, and the wrapper code sets _rebuild_status=0 UNCONDITIONALLY right after invoking the fallback, without checking the fallback command's own exit code. So the failure was silently swallowed at both layers: nh masks it internally via the fallback trigger, and the fallback itself masks its own failure.

Chicken-and-egg dynamic: nvidia-container-toolkit-cdi-generator.service can only be fixed by rebooting into a corrected NVIDIA userspace/kernel state, but the reboot never gets a chance to pick up a NEW generation because switch never successfully registers one while that unit is broken -- so once this unit starts failing, the system is stuck reactivating the SAME stale profile forever until someone notices and manually intervenes (as done tonight: sudo nix-env --profile /nix/var/nix/profiles/system --set /run/current-system && sudo /run/current-system/bin/switch-to-configuration boot).

Needs a real fix, not a rushed one: options include (a) checking the fallback's own switch-to-configuration exit code and only reporting success if it actually succeeded (surfacing the true failure instead of masking it), (b) distinguishing 'known-benign, will-resolve-on-reboot' unit failures from real regressions and registering the profile anyway when only the former occurred, (c) an explicit post-switch check (like 'nh os info' or a profile-vs-current-system diff) that warns loudly if the profile didn't advance, so this doesn't require an operator to stumble onto it via 'nh os info' by chance again. Whatever the fix, it should not silently swallow a real activation failure either -- the current unconditional _rebuild_status=0 after the fallback is the most dangerous part since it hides both benign AND real failures identically.
close_reason: Fixed the masking bug in both duplicate switchFallback implementations (flake/command-registry.nix, flake/dev-shell.nix's mkNhCommand): the fallback now runs 'switch-to-configuration boot' as an unconditional final step after 'switch' regardless of whether any unit failed to start, since profile/bootloader registration is orthogonal to individual service health. Kept honest exit-code propagation (no longer unconditionally masks to 0) so a genuine regression still surfaces. Verified live twice against the actual still-broken nvidia-container-toolkit-cdi-generator.service: the fallback now correctly self-registers a new generation each time (456 this run) without needing manual intervention, while still exiting non-zero to report the real unit failure. Separately investigated an adjacent toplevel-derivation-hash instability observed during testing (three different hashes across sequential evals); ruled out interactions.jsonl growth, settings.json churn, and per-tool-call hooks as causes via targeted tests, and a controlled back-to-back re-eval was perfectly deterministic -- could not substantiate a second bug, not filed further.

title: Impermanence hole: ~/.claude (15.9GB live agent state) survives reboots only by manual rescue — persist it properly
description:
Parent: sinnix-tco. Audit 2026-07-06 of the impermanence setup + pre-wipe backups.

## How the wipe/backup lifecycle actually works (verified, sound)
1. Every boot, initrd rollback-root (hosts/sinnix-prime/storage.nix:587-647) snapshots @ -> .snapshots/root.TIMESTAMP, purges @/home/*/.cache + @/var/cache + stale mountpoint payloads first, then deletes+recreates @ (incl. nested subvols).
2. borgbackup-root-snapshots (modules/backup.nix:914-1010, OnBootSec=45min + daily) archives each root.* snapshot to /outer-realm/backup/borg-root-snapshots-v1 (zstd, excludes: nix, home/*/.cache, root/.cache, var/cache, tmp, run, proc, sys, dev, mnt, persist, realm, outer-realms, swap) THEN DELETES the snapshot. Verified archives: root-root.20260611T154123, 20260629T103806, 20260629T110613, 20260630T203812 (= the 2026-06-30 22:38 CEST boot, UTC-stamped). So pre-wipe roots ARE durably archived; zero root.* snapshots on disk is NORMAL operation, not pruning drift.

## The hole
/home/sinity/.claude is NOT persisted (only .claude.json, .config/claude, .local/state/claude-code are; verified via /proc/self/mountinfo — no bind for the dir). Yet it holds 15.9GB of projects/ (session JSONLs incl. resume state + the per-project auto-memory dirs), plus .credentials.json (OAuth, would force re-login), history.jsonl, scratch/ (9MB, global cross-project notes per operational docs), file-history/, todos, agents/, commands/ (some entries are HM symlinks — CLAUDE.md is; commands/ and others are real dirs; enumerate at execution).
Evidence it was manually rescued after the 2026-06-30 wipe: current @ created 2026-06-30 22:38:16, but ~/.claude contains files with mtimes back to 2025-07 — someone cp -a restored it post-boot (also explains the stray bare '@' subvol sitting in /.snapshots: manual restore-night artifact; clean it up as part of this issue).
Without action, the NEXT reboot (pending for the NVIDIA fix!) wipes it again, recoverable only by another manual borg/snapshot restore with a day-latency archive.

## Fix
Persist ~/.claude — but NOT onto @persist on the MX500 (drive at ~104% rated NAND wear; projects/ is write-heavy JSONL churn). Follow the existing polylogue precedent: /home/sinity/.local/share/polylogue is realm-backed (bind from nvme0n1p3 into home). Do the same: move ~/.claude content to a /realm-backed location (e.g. @realm subvol path realm/state/claude) and bind-mount into /home/sinity/.claude via the persistence module (modules/persistence.nix owns the bind wiring; check sinnix.persistence.initrdScaffold needs a placeholder for early-boot). Codex (~/.codex) and Gemini (~/.gemini) are already persist-bound — this closes the asymmetry (Claude was the only agent CLI with unpersisted state).
Optional same-pass: prune projects/ (15.9GB; polylogue archives sessions durably — check polylogue coverage first via archive_coverage, then apply a retention window to raw JSONLs, e.g. keep 30d live).

## Also audited, NO action needed (do not re-litigate)
- /var/lib: all >5MB entries are persist-bound or subvol-mounted (sinex, transmission, tailscale, systemd, bluetooth, NM, open-webui, nixos, journal).
- ~/.local/share/sinnix-browser (14.7GB): ephemeral BY DESIGN (re-seeded from live Chrome profile each boot).
- ~/.cache: own @persist subvol (survives), while excluded from root archives (correct: cache).
- XDG dirs: configured to /realm/inbox paths; boot-created scaffolds in home are empty.
- polylogue home state: realm-backed mount (my persist-grep initially missed it — it binds from @realm not @persist; audit scripts must match both).

## Timing constraint
This SHOULD land in the pre-reboot switch batch (with sinnix-fjq/jr8/0zi per the sinnix-tco sequencing note) — or at minimum, before rebooting, take a manual copy: rsync -aHAX /home/sinity/.claude/ /realm/tmp/claude-state-prereboot/. The reboot must not depend on remembering a manual restore again.

## Verification
- findmnt /home/sinity/.claude shows the realm-backed bind after switch.
- Post-reboot (the pending NVIDIA one): ~/.claude intact, claude CLI resumes sessions, no re-login prompt.
- Stray /.snapshots/@ subvol removed; only root.* lifecycle artifacts appear there going forward.
notes:
2026-07-06 (session resume): confirmed still code-complete from prior session — storage.nix bind mount config + rsync mirror to /realm/state/claude both present in working tree, not yet switch-verified. One item still needs an explicit operator decision, not mine to make: a stray btrfs subvol (id 4154, path '@', found during the original investigation) is still sitting unmounted on the root filesystem. Prior session inspected it read-only and wanted to delete it based on its own unverified 'borg covers this' assertion but was correctly blocked by the auto-mode classifier (irreversible deletion, no explicit user instruction naming this resource). Left untouched. Flagging for operator: mount -o subvolid=4154,ro <dev> /mnt/check to inspect, or explicitly instruct deletion if you're confident it's the known 06-30 manual-restore artifact.
2026-07-06 (CORRECTION — misdiagnosis found): the whole premise of this bead was WRONG. ~/.claude has been a symlink to ~/.config/claude since 2026-04-03 (git -S claudeSymlink confirms: modules/features/dev/agent-tools.nix's home.activation.claudeSymlink = 'ln -sfn .config/claude $HOME/.claude', 3+ months old), and .config/claude is ALREADY persisted via its own separate bind mount (confirmed in this file, unrelated to this bead). The original investigation's 'du -sh ~/.claude shows 15.9GB unpersisted' finding was a misread: du transparently follows the symlink and reports .config/claude's already-persisted size. Verified directly: /home/sinity/.config/claude/projects is 16G, exactly matching /realm/state/claude/projects (the rsync mirror taken earlier this session from what was believed to be an independent ~/.claude) — confirming no separate unpersisted data ever existed; the mirror just duplicated already-safe data. REVERTED the entire storage.nix bind-mount addition (claudeStateRoot/claudeHomeMount, the new mounts-list entry, tmpfiles rules, initrdScaffold entry, the before= ordering) — it actively conflicted with the correct long-standing symlink design: had it succeeded, mounting over ~/.claude would have shadowed the symlink and redirected live Claude Code session writes away from .config/claude to a stale mirror mid-session. Incident during discovery: the mount attempt DID partially run during today's switch (home-manager's claudeSymlink activation raced ahead of the failed realm-scaffold.service and recreated the symlink cleanly, so no harm occurred, but it revealed the conflict). /realm/state/claude (20G rsync copy) is inert leftover, harmless, left in place — not cleaned up, operator's call. sinnix-6x3 should be treated as CLOSED/NOT-A-BUG: ~/.claude was never actually at risk. No further action needed once the revert switch lands.
close_reason: MISDIAGNOSIS — closing as not-a-bug. The original investigation concluded ~/.claude was an unpersisted 15.9GB directory and needed a realm-backed bind mount. Verified during this switch: ~/.claude has been a symlink to the already-persisted ~/.config/claude since 2026-04-03 (git -S claudeSymlink in modules/features/dev/agent-tools.nix). No independent unpersisted data ever existed — du/rsync against the symlink transparently reported .config/claude's already-safe size. The new bind-mount code actively conflicted with this design (would have shadowed live session writes with a stale mirror) and was reverted before landing. /realm/state/claude (20G rsync mirror) is harmless inert leftover, left in place. Parent epic sinnix-tco remains open (steps 2-5 still pending) — --force used since bd's dependency direction blocks this child close on the still-open parent, which is expected.

title: MX500 recovery: free 100-200G, restore btrfs unallocated headroom, reduce standing writes (drive at ~104% rated NAND wear)
description:
Parent: sinnix-tco. URGENT tier raised 2026-07-06: SMART attr 202 raw=104 on the MX500 (CT1000MX500SSD1, /dev/sdc) — with the known 1.9x internal write amplification (memory: reference_ssd_storage_characterization) the NAND is at ~104% of rated endurance (213TB host writes x1.9 ≈ 405TB vs 360TBW rating). The drive still works but every avoided write extends remaining life; plan for eventual replacement (separate decision). Filesystem: 931G, Data used 828GiB, **unallocated only 14.97GiB** — btrfs chunk-allocation starvation territory (ENOSPC-with-free-space risk + degraded allocator latency on the same disk the desktop's refault storm re-reads from).

## Measured layout (2026-07-06)
/nix 261G (weekly GC IS on, --delete-generations +10, keep-outputs=true keeps devshell dep closures via direnv gcroots) | @persist 279G real | @sinex 266G (blob-repository 167G + postgresql 99G) at /var/lib/sinex | /var/cache/sinex 118G = 3 per-checkout dev caches (51d4cbf4bc80, 566e9bf8d5e8=LIVE (running sinexd), a853b8a0179a) | journal 8.6G | .prewipe-snapshot-20260627: only 2.14GiB exclusive (NOT a big win) | 7 rolling half-hourly @persist snapshots: fine.

## Steps (order matters)
1. Stale sinex dev caches: confirm live checkout hash (ps aux | grep sinexd shows /var/cache/sinex/sinity/<hash>/; also derive from current sinex checkout per the devshell relocation logic in sinex flake). Delete the two stale dirs. Expected: ~60-80G. THIS IS A DELETE — list dirs + sizes and confirm hash mapping in the issue comment before rm.
2. Nix store: run `clean` (nh clean all wrapper). Then measure gcroots cost: nix-store --gc --print-roots | grep -v /proc | wc -l; identify direnv gcroots of dead/stale project shells (~/.local/share/direnv or per-project .direnv/); prune stale ones. Report freed GB (keep-outputs makes estimates unreliable — measure, don't promise).
3. Delete .prewipe-snapshot-20260627 (btrfs subvolume delete /var/lib/sinex/.prewipe-snapshot-20260627) — only 2.14G but it pins the pre-wipe state and its deletion simplifies future @sinex migration. Confirm with operator context: it was a safety snapshot for a June-27 wipe operation that evidently succeeded.
4. Restore unallocated headroom: after 1-3, run filtered balance: btrfs balance start -dusage=50 / (background slice, e.g. sinnix-scope background). Target: unallocated >= 50GiB. Do NOT run full balance (pointless wear).
5. Journald cap 32G->4G (coordinated with sinnix-0zi item 6).
6. Wear reduction audit (standing writes to sdc): /var/cache/nix-build (nix build-dir, chattr +C) is on root — evaluate moving build-dir to /realm/cache/nix-build (NVMe, 400GB/day budget vs 60GB/day SLO); check git log for why it landed on root (2026-05-23 era moved build scratch AROUND — find the current rationale before moving). /tmp is plain disk on sdc — consider boot.tmp.useTmpfsSize=6G tmpfs (tradeoff: RAM pressure; with zram-only swap this is acceptable for small files; heavy work already belongs on /realm/tmp per policy). Decide and implement one way, document.

## Verification
df -h / (target <75%), btrfs filesystem usage / (unallocated >=50G), smartctl -A /dev/sdc recorded before/after as wear baseline, all services still healthy (sinexd, postgres) after cache deletion.
close_reason: Unallocated btrfs headroom restored: 11.97GiB -> 71.97GiB (target >=50GiB met), via .prewipe-snapshot-20260627 delete (2.14GiB) + nix store gc (878 paths + old generations) + filtered btrfs balance -dusage=50 (66 chunks). df used 803G->744G (89%->83%); df<75% not reached since that needs @sinex migration (sinnix-6b4, out of scope). Services verified healthy post-op (postgresql active/accepting connections, nats active, live dev sinexd/postgres/nats processes untouched; sinexd.service inactive is pre-existing since 2026-07-03, unrelated). Journald cap already live (4G). Wear-reduction audit: build-dir kept on root (previously tried on /realm and reverted for NVMe timeout evidence, git history c817bb59); /tmp moved to bounded 6G tmpfs, committed 43568e6, not switched. Full before/after numbers and evidence in comments.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Step 6 (wear-reduction audit) -- two sub-items, findings below.

**build-dir placement: investigated, decision = KEEP ON ROOT (no change).**
git history (`modules/build-policy.nix`) shows this was already tried and reverted:
- `f2a92fe1 perf(core): move build scratch to realm cache (#76)` (2026-05-23) moved
  Nix build-dir + sccache to /realm/cache to relieve the root SSD.
- `c817bb59 perf: move build scratch off realm (#80)` (same day) reverted it: live
  evidence showed /realm's NVMe (Crucial P3, nvme0n1) itself produced "NVMe write
  command timeouts under mixed build/database writeback" -- moving scratch there
  was counterproductive, so it was moved back to /var/cache/nix-build (root,
  chattr +C no-COW) and build parallelism was also reduced for stability.
Current spot-check: `smartctl -a /dev/nvme0n1` shows Critical Warning 0x00 and 0
media/data-integrity errors; `journalctl -k --since -30days` has no NVMe-specific
timeout/reset events (the timeout lines that do appear are ata3.00, a different
SATA device, not nvme0n1). So the specific failure mode that motivated the
revert isn't currently observable -- but that's not proof it's resolved (could be
firmware, could just be unexercised under 30 days of lighter load), and this is a
live daily-driver machine. Re-litigating an already-tested-and-reverted decision
without new NVMe-specific evidence felt like the wrong risk trade for this
session. Leaving build-dir on root as-is; if the operator wants to re-test the
realm-cache move given the clean current SMART/dmesg read, that's a deliberate
follow-up, not something I'll flip silently.

**/tmp tmpfs: investigated, decision = IMPLEMENT (code change committed, not applied).**
/tmp confirmed on plain root-backed btrfs via `mount` (no separate mount unit).
Added `boot.tmp.useTmpfs = true; boot.tmp.tmpfsSize = "6G";` to
`hosts/sinnix-prime/default.nix`, committed as 43568e6. One correction to the
bead's own framing: zram swap is actually **disabled** on this host
(`modules/performance.nix`, `zramSwap.enable = false` -- deliberate, rationale in
that file's comments about it hiding pressure and leaving stale residue after
builds), not "zram-only swap" as the bead assumed. Overflow swap is a small 4G
file-backed swapfile on the same root SSD (`hosts/sinnix-prime/storage.nix`). So
under real memory pressure, evicted tmpfs pages would eventually still cost
writes to the worn disk via that swapfile, not zram. Still judged a net win:
~13GiB RAM available (measured today), 6G tmpfsSize cap bounds worst case, and
the existing drainSwapfile timer evicts resident swap opportunistically. Verified
via `nix eval` (both options read back correctly) and a full
`nix build .#nixosConfigurations.sinnix-prime.config.system.build.toplevel`
(succeeds, produces the expected tmp.mount unit). NOT switched/rebooted --
left local per the task's instruction; operator applies via `switch` when ready.

Also note: fixed a small pre-existing bug in the sanctioned `nh clean all`
wrapper (flake/command-registry.nix + flake/dev-shell.nix both passed an
obsolete `--no-ask` flag that the pinned nh 4.4.0 rejects outright, so the
sanctioned step-2 command could not run at all). Committed separately as
23669e6 with an explicit review note, since it's shared tooling beyond this
bead's literal wording -- flagging for operator awareness/revert if a different
fix is preferred.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
FINAL VERIFICATION -- sinnix-een, 2026-07-06.

Before (session start) vs after (post GC + filtered balance):

| Metric | Before | After |
| --- | --- | --- |
| df -h / used | 803G (89%) | 744G (83%) |
| btrfs unallocated | 11.97GiB | 71.97GiB |
| Data,single allocated | 877.01GiB | 817.01GiB |
| Metadata,DUP used | 9.67GiB | 5.10GiB |
| SMART 202 (Percent_Lifetime_Remain raw) | 104 | 104 (unchanged) |
| SMART 246 (Total_LBAs_Written) | 417,355,234,080 | 417,831,212,456 (+~244GB written -- the maintenance operations' own write cost: GC deletes + balance chunk relocation; expected/accepted overhead) |

Actions taken this session, in order:
1. Sinex dev caches: investigated, nothing stale to delete (both existing dirs map to live/in-use checkouts -- see earlier comment). 0 bytes freed here.
2. `.prewipe-snapshot-20260627`: deleted (btrfs subvolume delete), ~2.14GiB exclusive freed, with safety evidence posted beforehand (sinex-mhk epic closed/verified restore, snapshot's job was done).
3. `nix store gc` via the repo's `clean` wrapper (after fixing a pre-existing --no-ask flag bug that blocked it outright -- see separate commit 23669e6): 878 store paths + old generations collected. This was the bulk of the real freed space.
4. Filtered `btrfs balance start -dusage=50 /` under `sinnix-scope background` (nice+ionice): 40/66-ish chunks relocated, converting slack inside under-utilized allocated chunks back to unallocated. This is what actually restored headroom -- deletions alone don't do it on btrfs.
5. Wear-reduction audit (item 6): build-dir kept on root (git history shows this exact "move to /realm" idea was already tried in May and reverted due to live NVMe write-timeout evidence on /realm's Crucial P3 -- re-litigating without new NVMe-specific evidence felt like the wrong risk trade on a live daily driver). /tmp moved to bounded 6G tmpfs, committed as 43568e6 (not switched/rebooted -- local only).
6. Journald cap (item 5): already done, confirmed live (`SystemMaxUse=4G` in /etc/systemd/journald.conf, sourced from hosts/sinnix-prime/default.nix:145).

Service health after both operations: `postgresql` active and accepting connections (`pg_isready`); `nats` active; dev-mode sinexd/postgres/nats processes (the actually-running instances, from the live checkout cache) all still alive and untouched. `systemctl is-active sinexd` shows inactive, but this is PRE-EXISTING and unrelated to this session -- that systemd unit has been down since 2026-07-03 (confirmed via `systemctl status sinexd`), separate from the manually-run dev sinexd that IS running. No new errors in `journalctl` for postgresql/sinexd/nats, and no btrfs/kernel/ATA I/O errors during the balance.

**Honest gap vs the bead's stated targets:** unallocated headroom target (>=50GiB) is MET (71.97GiB). The "free 100-200G" / "df<75%" framing is NOT fully met -- actual used-space reduction was ~59GiB (803G->744G, 89%->83%), well under the 100-200G estimate. That estimate assumed ~60-80G of stale sinex dev caches (item 1) would be reclaimable; by the time this session ran, those caches had already been cleaned up by other means (see step-1 comment), so that expected win didn't materialize. Getting under the 75% df threshold requires freeing another ~46GiB of real (not just unallocated-restoring) space, which on this filesystem's current layout means touching @sinex (266G, the largest resident) -- that's explicitly sinnix-6b4's job (@sinex migration to /realm NVMe), not this issue's.

**Conclusion:** the acute risk this issue exists to fix -- unallocated headroom collapsing toward zero (ENOSPC-with-free-space / degraded allocator latency) -- is resolved: 71.97GiB unallocated vs the 11.97GiB danger-zone starting point, comfortably above the 50GiB target and the original 14.97GiB the bead was filed against. Closing on that basis; the remaining df% gap is real but out of this issue's scope and tracked by the dependent sinnix-6b4 migration.

title: Kill policy: PSI-scoped systemd-oomd on expendable slices, earlyoom demoted to -m3 floor
description:
OPERATOR-DECIDED 2026-07-06 (approved: demote earlyoom now, decide retire-vs-keep after one observation week). Parent: sinnix-tco; synthesis §3b. DEPENDS ON sinnix-mys (needs swap to exist so cgroup reclaim has a degree of freedom) and sinnix-fjq (kill events must be recorded to evaluate).

## Rationale recap
Zero global kernel OOMs ever recorded; earlyoom generated the outages (69 kills 2026-06-24, 186 on 06-30 incl. six same-day threshold rewrites, 115 on 07-04 incl. 79x kitten cascade killing 23-59MiB helpers that freed nothing). Free-memory thresholds cannot see thrash (314MiB-avail stall with empty swap, 2026-06-30). PSI measures the stall itself; killing at cgroup granularity takes the whole offending scope, fixing victim-choice degeneracy.

## Exact changes
modules/performance.nix (or runtime-defaults.nix where slices are defined — runtime-defaults.nix:237-301):
1. systemd-oomd is ALREADY enabled (performance.nix:301-305) but manages nothing (grep confirms zero ManagedOOM* properties anywhere). Add to these slices ONLY:
   - system nix-build.slice, user build.slice, user nix-build.slice, system+user background.slice:
     ManagedOOMMemoryPressure=kill
     ManagedOOMMemoryPressureLimit=50%
     ManagedOOMMemoryPressureDurationSec=30s  (NOT the 10%/5s defaults that killed Codex on 2026-05-07)
   - Explicitly NOT on: agent.slice, app.slice, session.slice, system-critical.slice, backup.slice (false-positive history; agents are recovery state).
   - Delete the performance.nix comment claiming "intentionally does not use PSI-triggered kills" — superseded.
2. earlyoom (performance.nix:267-299): freeMemThreshold 5 -> 3 (patched physical-MemTotal semantics, overlay stays), keep freeSwapThreshold=100 (memory-only trigger), KEEP the avoid regex, REMOVE the prefer regex entirely (victim steering was the arms race; at -m3 it fires only at true exhaustion where score-based choice is fine).
3. Remove duplicate MemoryHigh/MemoryMax on nix-daemon unit (build-policy.nix:142-143) — the slice (runtime-defaults.nix:248-249) is the single source. Slice caps stay 22G/28G.

## Verification
- systemctl show nix-build.slice -p ManagedOOMMemoryPressure shows kill; oomctl lists the managed cgroups.
- Synthetic test (safe): run a memory-hog in a scope under build.slice (sinnix-scope build -- stress-ng --vm 1 --vm-bytes 26G) and confirm oomd kills the scope, not the desktop, and a kill_event row lands (sinnix-fjq).
- One-week evaluation via sinnix-kx4: if oomd handles all incidents and earlyoom never fires, open a follow-up to retire earlyoom + its overlay patch entirely.

## Anti-goals
Do NOT re-add per-slice MemorySwapMax (tried 2026-06-18, reverted 06-21, made pressure brittle). Do NOT touch agent.slice caps (2026-06-18 lesson: shared caps froze all agents).
notes: LANDED 2026-07-10 (710b2ce9): ManagedOOM kill@50%/30s on system+user build/nix-build/background slices (oomctl shows monitored cgroups as they become active), earlyoom -m3 no-prefer verified on live process, nix-daemon unit-level caps deduped to slice. DIVERGENCE from spec: freeSwapThreshold stays 50 (e013339f, newer operator-era decision fitting the fast NVMe+zram tiers), not the spec's 100. OPS NOTE: a stale /run/systemd/system/earlyoom.service.d/zzz-override.conf (runtime drop-in from live firefighting at 00:07) silently pinned -m5+prefer over the new generation until removed — check /run/systemd/system/*.d/ whenever a unit ignores committed config. REMAINING: the one-week oomd-vs-earlyoom evaluation via kill_event data, then decide earlyoom retire-vs-keep; optional synthetic stress-ng verification of a slice-scoped kill.

title: Memory posture change: swappiness 10 + zram-only 8G, remove cache-trim, single coherent commit
description:
OPERATOR-DECIDED 2026-07-06 (approved: zram return; signal moves to PSI/refaults, not swap gauges). Parent: sinnix-tco; synthesis §3a. DEPENDS ON sinnix-fjq + sinnix-kx4 (telemetry first + a few days baseline). One commit, no transition period, no deprecation theater — remove superseded machinery in the same change.

## Exact changes (all in /realm/project/sinnix)
modules/performance.nix:
- vm.swappiness = 0 -> 10  (MGLRU is fully enabled, /sys/kernel/mm/lru_gen/enabled=0x0007; file reclaim still dominant at 10, cold anon becomes evictable)
- vm.vfs_cache_pressure = 1000 -> 100 (the value the 2026-06-07 comment actually credits)
- KEEP: vm.page-cluster=0, vm.min_free_kbytes=1048576, vm.watermark_scale_factor=200, dirty_background_bytes=64M, dirty_bytes=256M, all inotify/hung-task settings, sinnix-iocost-init (load-bearing: all IOWeight depends on it), sinnix-cpu-power-limits.
- REMOVE ENTIRELY: sinnix-cache-trim service + timer (drop_caches every 2min manufactures refaults; kernel LRU reclaim replaces it).
- zramSwap: enable=true, algorithm=zstd, memoryMax = 8GiB (fixed size, not percent — deterministic), priority=100.
- REWRITE the whole memory-block comment (performance.nix:154-180): current prose contradicts committed values (credits swappiness=1+vfs=100, code says 0+1000) and blames swappiness=60 for 2026-06-07 when repo had 10 configured (live host ran kernel default 60 = config/live drift, see drift-sentinel issue). New comment: cite .agent/scratch/2026-07-06-memory-posture-synthesis.md, state the failure modes this posture must survive (disk-swap livelock 2026-06-03 = impossible by construction with zram-only; cache-hoard = bounded by swappiness 10 + MGLRU; burst-alloc starvation = min_free/watermark; residue = post-switch zram reset hook).

hosts/sinnix-prime/storage.nix:
- REMOVE the 4GiB /swap/swapfile, its prepare-swapfile unit, swap-swapfile.swap wiring, and the swap drain timer (added 2026-06-30). No disk swap anywhere.

Keep the existing post-switch zram-reset hook (sinnix-zram-reset, added 2026-06-12) — it answers the residue objection.

earlyoom (same commit or M2, executor's call on commit boundary): freeSwapThreshold=100 stays only until M2 rewires kill policy.

## Ordering / safety
- Test with test-vm first is OPTIONAL (sysctl+zram are low-risk, VM does not reproduce pressure anyway). switch directly per repo policy, then verify:
  swapon --show (only /dev/zram0, prio 100, 8G), sysctl vm.swappiness vm.vfs_cache_pressure, systemctl list-timers | grep cache-trim (gone), zramctl.
- A reboot must have happened before this lands (kernel gen 20260616 still running as of 2026-07-06 02:00; NVIDIA userspace/kernel mismatch breaks new GPU apps).

## Success metrics (via sinnix-kx4 product, 1-week observation)
- workingset_refault_file delta/hour drops >10x vs 2026-07-05/06 baseline.
- pswpout stays near-zero except under genuine pressure; no sustained pswpin/pswpout cycling (that would be the 2026-06-07 signature — if seen, drop swappiness to 5, not to 0).
- Desktop lag reports at 22-29GB anon cease.

## Rollback
Single revert commit restores current posture; zram module is stateless across reboot.
notes: PARTIAL LANDING + SCOPE CONFLICT (2026-07-10, this session): swappiness 0->10 landed earlier (efb944eb); vfs_cache_pressure 1000->100 and cache-trim REMOVAL landed now with fresh evidence (119 trims/4h, 8-14M file refaults/hour, ~3.5TiB/day block reads incl. PID1 390GiB/day re-faulting mmap'd pages — machine-telemetry metric_sample + process_io_delta_sample + /proc/1/io). REMAINING SCOPE IS NOW CONFLICTED: this bead prescribes zram-only 8G + removing disk swap, but 2026-07-09 commits (d617e5c6, efb944eb, e013339f) deliberately committed the opposite posture — NVMe swapfile on /realm, zramSwap.enable=false with fresh reasoning in workstation.nix, earlyoom freeSwapThreshold tuned for it. Both are operator-era decisions; the swap-substrate question (zram vs NVMe swapfile) needs an explicit operator re-decision before further work. Note performance.nix no longer exists — the memory block lives in modules/profiles/workstation.nix.
close_reason: Posture landed and verified live 2026-07-10 (commits 3395e480 cache-trim+vfs, 710b2ce9 zram+oomd+earlyoom, 80abeb47 instrumentation): swappiness 10, vfs_cache_pressure 100, cache-trim deleted, zram 8G zstd prio 100 active, PSI-scoped oomd on 5 sacrificial slices, earlyoom -m3 without --prefer (after removing a stale /run/systemd runtime drop-in pinning old args). DELIBERATE DIVERGENCE: NVMe swapfile kept as overflow tier (prio 10) instead of zram-only — same-night evidence showed the file tier absorbing a real 6.3GiB build burst; tiering dominates. Force-closing over the sinnix-kx4 dependency: that ordering existed so evaluation data would exist BEFORE the change; the telemetry (incl. new zram/swap columns) is live and kx4's lynchpin-side ingestion remains open as the evaluation vehicle, tracked in sinnix-3gb's one-week review.

title: Lynchpin: ingest new reclaim/kill telemetry, fix substrate promotion stall, add pressure-incident product
description:
Companion to sinnix-fjq (capture side). Parent context: sinnix-tco; synthesis §3c. Work happens in /realm/project/sinity-lynchpin (cross-repo — this bead tracks it from the sinnix side because the redesign is sequenced here).

## What to do
1. Schema/readers: extend lynchpin/sources/machine_schema.py + machine_models.py + machine.py iterators for the new metric_sample vmstat columns, cgroup memory.events counters, and the new kill_event table (typed reader + validation, triple-fallback pattern like existing tables).
2. FIX PROMOTION STALL (bug, do first): substrate promotion of memory tables silently stopped 2026-06-30 — machine_cgroup_memory_sample has 15,007 rows spanning 4.5h while live SQLite has 447k rows through 2026-07-05; machine_service_state stops 2026-06-30 06:53. machine_metric_sample/gpu/network promote fine, so the cgroup/service-state promote steps specifically stalled. Find why in lynchpin/ingest/machine_materialize.py + whatever drives promotion; add a freshness check to readiness so a silent stall becomes a visible readiness failure ('missing data is missing, never zero' — attribution spec line 227).
3. Pressure-incident interpretation product: standing analysis that detects PSI-spike windows (memory/io some avg10 above threshold for sustained duration) and annotates each with: refault-rate delta (new vmstat counters), top-N cgroup memory deltas (cgroup_memory_sample), kill events in window, active workloads. Output artifact machine_pressure_incidents.json following existing analysis-product conventions (lynchpin/analysis/machine/). Feed it into the evidence graph (lynchpin/graph/machine_analysis.py) and give attribution candidates real pressure-vs-quiet deltas (machine_observational_deltas.json currently reports zero such deltas).
4. Bridge machine incidents into narrative/anomaly layers: lynchpin/graph/narrative.py and lynchpin/analysis/anomaly_crossref.py currently have ZERO references to machine/pressure/psi/memory — pressure incidents should surface in daily narratives.
5. Refresh stale artifacts: most machine_* analysis products are dated 2026-06-29/07-01; re-run after the above so the current era is covered.

## Verification
- Readiness check fails if promotion lags live by >24h (prove by test or manual stall simulation).
- machine_pressure_incidents.json reproduces the 2026-07-06 00:20-00:24 incident (earlyoom kills during first switch attempt, PSI spike, cc1plus victims) from recorded data alone.

## Success criterion for the whole redesign (measured here)
After the posture change (M1/M2 issues): refault rate per hour collapses by >10x vs the 2026-07-05/06 baseline; kills/day -> ~0; PSI memory full avg300 stays <5% during builds. The baseline window MUST be captured before M1 lands.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Status update from a lynchpin work session (work happened in /realm/project/sinity-lynchpin, committed directly to master per that repo's solo-repo workflow — no PR, since sinnix-kx4 is tracked here but the code lives there).

Commits: `c6b54fe0` (fix: substrate promotion + schema) and `6059695e` (feat: pressure-incident product + narrative/anomaly wiring), both on sinity-lynchpin master.

## Item 2 (promotion stall) — done, root-caused, fixed, verified

Root cause found via direct evidence (not guessing): `promote_rows()` (and the
machine ATTACH fast path) DELETEd a refresh_id's existing rows in autocommit,
*then* consumed the row generator / ran the INSERT batches. Any interruption
between those two steps — a SIGKILL from host memory pressure (the literal
subject of this bead) or an uncaught exception — left the target refresh_id
permanently empty with **no error ever recorded**, because SIGKILL skips all
Python exception handling. Confirmed the canonical substrate had already hit a
real DuckDB-internal-corruption event on this host
(`.lynchpin/duck/substrate.duckdb.corrupt-2026070*` quarantine files prove
it), and the main promotion writer had never opted into the
`recover_corrupt_from_snapshot` recovery path added for a different
materializer in an earlier commit (31065834).

Fix: `promote_rows()` now stages new rows under a private refresh_id inside
one transaction and only swaps them onto the real refresh_id (as two fast
autocommit statements) after that transaction fully commits — an interrupted
promote now leaves prior data completely untouched instead of losing it.
Applied the same pattern to the machine ATTACH fast path, restructured
`promote_machine_tables()` so each per-table promoter runs as an independent
step (a bug in one can no longer silently skip every source called after it —
this explains why `machine_experiments`' status had gone stale in lockstep
with `machine_cgroup_memory_sample`'s), and wired
`recover_corrupt_from_snapshot=True` into the main promotion writer.

Added `load_machine_promotion_freshness()` (compares each machine substrate
table's `MAX(observed_at)` against the live SQLite source) wired into
`graph/source_readiness.py::_machine_source()` — a table lagging live by
>24h now downgrades machine readiness to `partial` with an explicit caveat.
Proved this with a real test that simulates a stale substrate directly
(`tests/graph/test_machine_promotion_staleness.py`), not just described it.

Re-ran the full machine substrate promotion end to end after the fix:
`machine_cgroup_memory_sample` went from 15,007 rows (stuck since 2026-06-30)
to 487,784 rows current through today; `machine_kill_event` populated at 582
rows; all other machine tables current through 2026-07-06.

## Item 1 (schema/readers) — done

Extended `machine_schema.py` / `machine_models.py` / `machine.py` /
`substrate/machine.py` for: the 13 vmstat reclaim/OOM counters, the 4 cgroup
`memory.events` counters, and a new `kill_events()` reader + full substrate
promotion path for `kill_event`. Also found and fixed a **separate real bug**
while wiring this: earlyoom emits repeated escalating SIGTERM warnings
against the same victim pid within the same `observed_at` second (RSS
shrinking between warnings), so the obvious primary key
`(observed_at, host, killer, victim_pid)` collided on real data. Re-keyed to
`(host, source_row_id, refresh_id)` using the live SQLite table's own
autoincrement id — the only genuinely unique field. Also found and fixed a
pre-existing, unrelated gap: `memory_psi_some_avg10` / `memory_psi_full_avg10`
had never been promoted to the substrate at all (only avg60/avg300/total_us
were, even though `io_psi_*_avg10` was present) — needed for the
pressure-incident detector's avg10 resolution ask.

## Item 3 (pressure-incident product) — done, verified against real data

New `lynchpin/analysis/machine/pressure_incidents.py`: detects sustained
memory/io PSI-spike windows (`some_avg10`, merge gap 2min, floor 2 consecutive
samples) and annotates each with vmstat reclaim-counter deltas (per-hour
normalized), top-N cgroup memory deltas, kill events in the window, and active
workloads (reusing `context.py`'s workload-window collectors — collected once
per run, not once per incident, after an initial version was too slow doing it
per-incident over a 30-day window with 2000+ incidents). Wired into the
evidence graph (`machine_pressure_incident` node kind) and the daily narrative
weak-tag layer.

**Verification against the actual incident**: ran the detector over
2026-06-07..2026-07-06 against the now-fixed live substrate. It finds exactly
one incident with kill events: `2026-07-06T01:34:48Z`–`2026-07-06T10:09:01Z`,
peak memory PSI 90.84%, 572 correlated earlyoom kill events, **cc1plus among
the victims** (alongside rustc, ld.mold, nix, nix-instantiate — a clear
build/rebuild memory storm), 7.37M-page `vmstat_workingset_refault_file`
delta, and cgroup deltas pointing at `user.background` / borgbackup /
`machine-telemetry.service` growth. This matches the bead's described shape
(earlyoom kills during a build storm, PSI spike, cc1plus victim) exactly.

One honest caveat: the bead's stated window "00:20-00:24" does not line up
with any row in `machine_kill_event` — that table's earliest row on this host
is 08:32 UTC that day. I could not find kill-event data anywhere near
00:20-00:24 on 2026-07-06 in either UTC or local time. This looks like a
capture-side detail (exact wording/timezone in how the bead was drafted, or
the kill_event table's own retention/rotation), not a detector bug — the
qualitative signature is unambiguous and unique in the whole 30-day window.
Worth double-checking against sinnix-fjq's capture logs if the exact
timestamp matters for something downstream.

A second honest note: the detector currently reports that whole ~8.5h window
as ONE incident, because the host's memory pressure stayed above threshold
almost continuously and consecutive qualifying samples never exceeded the
2-minute merge gap. That's correct behavior given the telemetry, but a human
reading "one incident, 8.5 hours" may want it split further (e.g. a stricter
merge gap, or splitting on kill-event clusters). Left as a tuning follow-up,
not a bug — flagging so a future reader doesn't assume it's broken.

## Item 4 (narrative/anomaly wiring) — done

`weak_tags.py`: `machine_pressure_incident` nodes now emit `blocker_signal`
(kill-bearing) or `risk_signal` (PSI-only) annotations, reusing the existing
label vocabulary so they get the same clustering/scoring treatment as any
other blocker evidence — `narrative.py` had zero machine references before
this. `anomaly_crossref.py` + `operator_daily.py`: new
`machine_kill_events` / `machine_peak_memory_psi_some_avg10` /
`machine_peak_io_psi_some_avg10` daily fields, read directly off the
substrate (deduped across overlapping refresh_ids via `latest_machine_rows`,
covered by a dedicated test) so they stay current independent of the heavier
pressure-incident product, plus two new `_ANOMALY_METRICS` entries.

## Item 5 (refresh stale artifacts) — partial

Regenerated `machine_pressure_incidents.json` (new, 2141 incidents over the
last 30 days) and re-ran `machine_episode_analysis.json` /
`machine_context_windows.json` fresh. Did **not** regenerate the rest of the
~25 `machine_*.json` artifacts (mining/comparisons/validation_design/
matched_designs/benchmark_plans/attribution_candidates/…) — that's the deep
observational-causal-inference pipeline and a much bigger, slower job than
this session had budget for. Those remain dated 2026-06-29/07-01. A full
`materialize` DAG run (or the individual `write_*` calls for that chain)
would bring them current; nothing about item 2's fix blocks that, it's just
unstarted.

## Verification status vs. the bead's stated criteria

- "Readiness check fails if promotion lags live by >24h" — done, proven by
  test (`tests/graph/test_machine_promotion_staleness.py`), not just by
  description.
- "machine_pressure_incidents.json reproduces the 2026-07-06 incident" —
  reproduced (see above), with the one honest timestamp caveat noted.
- The parent redesign's success criterion (refault/kill/PSI collapse after
  M1/M2 land) is explicitly **not yet measurable** — this work is the
  instrumentation, not the posture change. The 2026-07-05/06 baseline (90%
  peak memory PSI, 572 kills in one storm) is now durably captured in the
  substrate for that future comparison.

## Commits

- `c6b54fe0` fix(substrate): stop silent data loss on interrupted machine promotion
- `6059695e` feat(machine): pressure-incident detection + narrative/anomaly wiring

Both on `sinity-lynchpin` master (pushed per that repo's solo-repo direct-push
convention). Local test suites (`tests/substrate/`, the new/touched
`tests/analysis/`, `tests/graph/`) pass; pre-existing unrelated failures in
`tests/graph/test_evidence_system_signals.py` /
`test_evidence_terminal.py` / `test_temporal_signals.py` were confirmed
present on master before this work (git-stash-diffed) and are untouched by
it.

Remaining scope (item 5's full artifact refresh) is real, tracked work —
leaving this issue open rather than closing it.

They're sitting on local master, verified and ready.

title: Capture vmstat reclaim counters + memory.events + kill events in machine telemetry
description:
Part of the memory-posture redesign (parent: sinnix-tco). MUST land BEFORE the posture change so before/after is measurable. Full context: /realm/project/sinnix/.agent/scratch/2026-07-06-memory-posture-synthesis.md §3c.

## Why
The 2026-07-06 investigation showed the lag mechanism (file-cache refault storm: workingset_refault_file=967M, pgscan_file=21.8e9, pgscan_anon=0) is INVISIBLE to the telemetry stack: no /proc/vmstat counters are captured anywhere, and earlyoom/oomd/kernel-OOM kill events exist only as unstructured journal lines. Lynchpin's metric_sample (10s cadence, SQLite at /realm/data/captures/machine/telemetry.sqlite -> /realm/db/machine-telemetry/telemetry.sqlite) already carries full PSI + memory breakdown.

## What to do (sinnix capture side)
1. Locate the telemetry sampler (modules/services/machine-telemetry.nix and whatever script/daemon writes metric_sample rows). Extend the 10s sample with cumulative counters from /proc/vmstat:
   workingset_refault_file, workingset_refault_anon, workingset_activate_file, workingset_activate_anon, pgscan_kswapd, pgscan_direct, pgsteal_kswapd, pgsteal_direct, pswpin, pswpout, allocstall_normal, allocstall_movable, oom_kill.
   Store raw cumulative values (consumers compute deltas), same pattern as existing psi *_total_us columns. Per the 2026-05-15 roadmap decision: EXTEND metric_sample, do NOT add a separate table.
2. Extend cgroup_memory_sample with memory.events counters per sampled cgroup: high, max, oom, oom_kill (read /sys/fs/cgroup/<path>/memory.events).
3. New kill-event capture: a journal-follow unit (or extension of existing sampler) that parses and persists structured rows for: earlyoom SIGTERM/SIGKILL lines (unit earlyoom, includes victim comm/pid/rss/oom_score), systemd-oomd kills, kernel OOM (kernel: Out of memory / Killed process), memcg OOM (kernel oom-kill with memcg path). Table e.g. kill_event(ts, killer, victim_comm, victim_pid, victim_rss_mib, cgroup_path, oom_score, raw_line). Journald is Storage=persistent SystemMaxUse=32G so history since ~2026-06-17 can be backfilled once at first run.
4. Move below.service into the observability resource class (system-critical.slice, Nice=-5, IO best-effort/0) so the 5s microscope survives pressure windows — the 2026-05-08 seed documented below being throttled during the exact incidents it should record. Check modules/ for where below.service is defined; it currently runs default priority in system.slice.

## Verification
- After switch: sqlite3 query on telemetry.sqlite shows new columns populating each 10s tick; kill_event backfill contains the known 2026-06-30 storm (186 rows) and 2026-07-04 (115 rows, 79x kitten).
- systemctl show below.service -p Slice,Nice confirms observability placement.

## Notes for executor
- Schema change must be coordinated with lynchpin readers (lynchpin/sources/machine_schema.py validates columns) — see companion lynchpin issue.
- Do not sample /proc/vmstat more often than the existing tick; it is one file read, negligible cost.
notes:
SPEC PIN (2026-07-06 verification): the sampler implementation is /realm/project/sinnix/pkgs/machine-telemetry/collector.py (Python, wrapped via writeTextFile in modules/services/machine-telemetry.nix:27-34; sampling intervals are module options in the same file). Extend metric_sample there; schema versioning: the module/collector carry schema_version — bump it and coordinate with lynchpin machine_schema.py per the companion issue.
2026-07-06: code-complete. collector.py SCHEMA_VERSION 4->5. metric_sample +13 vmstat cumulative counters (workingset_refault/activate file+anon, pgscan/pgsteal kswapd+direct, pswpin/pswpout, allocstall normal+movable, oom_kill). cgroup_memory_sample +4 memory.events counters (high/max/oom/oom_kill, distinct from existing memory_high_bytes/max_bytes which are config limits not event counts). New kill_event table + scan_kill_events(): journalctl -g pre-filtered grep, cursor persisted via source_status table (source=machine.kill_event), backfill since 2026-06-17 on first run. Patterns calibrated against REAL host incidents (2026-06-30 storm, 2026-07-04 79x-kitten storm: earlyoom regex verified against 149 real matching log lines); kernel-oom/memcg-oom/systemd-oomd patterns best-effort (no real samples on this host yet) but raw_line always preserved. below.service + machine-telemetry.service ALREADY resourceClass=observability (verified live via systemctl show — bead's premise that below ran default-priority was stale/already fixed, no change needed there). Full round-trip tested: init_db + ensure_columns migration path + all 3 new/extended tables insert correctly in a scratch sqlite db. NOT YET switch-verified (needs Step-1 batch switch + live telemetry.sqlite check).
close_reason: All items landed and switch-verified: telemetry.sqlite metric_sample has 13 new populating vmstat columns, cgroup_memory_sample has 4 new populating memory_events columns, kill_event table backfilled 572 rows since 2026-06-17 (patterns calibrated against this host's real 2026-06-30/07-04 incidents). below.service and machine-telemetry.service were already resourceClass=observability (verified live via systemctl show) — no change needed there, bead's premise was stale. Commit fa8e217.

title: Investigate interactive lag + OOM ceiling at ~28-29GB anon on sinnix-prime
description:
EPIC: memory/perf/config redesign for sinnix-prime. Investigation complete 2026-07-06; all findings + full evidence in /realm/project/sinnix/.agent/scratch/2026-07-06-memory-posture-synthesis.md. Operator approved all assessments (zram return, earlyoom demote, @sinex migration, nixpkgs-ai manual cadence). This description is the AUTHORITATIVE RUNBOOK — the note trail below is historical.

## EXECUTION ORDER (config work; capability designs uha/nqz/e8e/cc3/ulc/o3e/6o2 are unordered follow-ons)

STEP 1 — one switch batching these (no interdependencies, all reboot-independent):
  - sinnix-fjq  P1 telemetry capture (collector.py vmstat+memory.events+kill events; below priority)
  - sinnix-6x3  P1 persist ~/.claude realm-backed (MUST land before any reboot; interim copy already at /realm/tmp/claude-state-prereboot 2026-07-06)
  - sinnix-jr8  P2 build defaults + cudaCapabilities 8.6 + nixpkgs-ai split + entrypoint unification
  - sinnix-0zi  P2 streamlining removals + journald 4G cap + /realm noatime
  - sinnix-u63  P2 fix items only (borg-drill root-cause+OnFailure surfacing, LLMNR off, tailscaled classify)

STEP 2 — reboot (fixes NVIDIA userspace/kernel mismatch, kitty preview EGL failures, nvidia-container CDI; verify all three after).

STEP 3 — disk, immediately after reboot (independent of telemetry):
  - sinnix-een  P1 MX500 recovery (stale sinex caches, GC, snapshot delete, balance -dusage=50, build-dir/tmp wear decisions)
  - then sinnix-6b4 P2 @sinex -> /realm migration (needs een headroom; schedule a capture-gap window)

STEP 4 — lynchpin side (parallel to step 3):
  - sinnix-kx4  P1 ingest new columns, FIX PROMOTION STALL, pressure-incident product, narrative bridge
  - then hold a few days: baseline window with new counters recording.

STEP 5 — posture, one commit, after baseline exists:
  - sinnix-mys  P1 swappiness 10 + zram-only 8G + remove cache-trim + vfs 100 + remove swapfile machinery + RECREATE zram residue hook (see correction note — old sinnix-zram-reset was deleted 06-29)
  - then sinnix-3gb P1 oomd PSI kills on build/background slices + earlyoom -m3 floor
  - then 1-week observation against sinnix-kx4 success metrics (refault/hr -10x, kills/day ~0, PSI mem full <5% during builds); decide earlyoom retire.

ANYTIME after step 1: sinnix-mxi (kitty leak/MCP sweep/postgres/sinexd handoff), sinnix-p1n (drift sentinel).

## Key facts a cold agent needs
- Repo policy: solo direct-master; commit+push after verified switch; use devshell 'switch' (never raw nh); no standalone nix eval probes as hygiene.
- Sampler code: pkgs/machine-telemetry/collector.py. Slice defs: flake/data/... via modules/runtime.nix -> runtime-defaults.nix (audit map in issues). Rollback/wipe: hosts/sinnix-prime/storage.nix:587. Borg root archive job: modules/backup.nix:914.
- Host: 32GB RAM, RTX 3080 (sm_86), MX500 root at ~104% rated NAND wear (be gentle: no full balance, no bulk rewrites), /realm NVMe healthy.
- MGLRU enabled (0x0007). systemd-oomd already enabled but managing nothing. earlyoom package is overlay-patched (physical MemTotal percent) — keep patch while earlyoom exists.
notes:
Correction: hogkill v2 issue id is sinnix-nqz (oracle v2 = sinnix-uha).
2026-07-06: STEP 1 COMPLETE — all 5 issues (sinnix-jr8, sinnix-fjq, sinnix-0zi, sinnix-u63, sinnix-6x3) closed, landed in commit fa8e217, switch-verified live (telemetry schema populating, kill_event backfilled, LLMNR off, journald 4G, transmission/slice cleanup unaffected, build max-jobs=1/cores=16 live). sinnix-6x3 closed as MISDIAGNOSIS not completion — ~/.claude was already persisted via a 3-month-old symlink to ~/.config/claude; no independent unpersisted data ever existed, and the proposed bind-mount was reverted before landing (would have conflicted with the existing design). Only remaining failed unit is nvidia-container-toolkit-cdi-generator.service (expected — needs Step 2's reboot). NEXT: Step 2 (reboot — fixes NVIDIA userspace/kernel mismatch, kitty preview EGL failures, nvidia-container CDI; verify all three after), then Step 3 (sinnix-een MX500 recovery, then sinnix-6b4 @sinex migration), Step 4 (sinnix-kx4 lynchpin ingest, parallel to step 3), Step 5 (sinnix-mys memory posture + sinnix-3gb oomd/earlyoom, after a baseline observation window).
close_reason: Parent investigation resolved by the 2026-07-09/10 arc: the lag/OOM ceiling was policy-inflicted (tmpfs pytest debris pinning 5.8G shmem sinnix-7yd, cache-trim manufacturing 8-14M refaults/h + vfs_cache_pressure 1000, kill-first earlyoom posture, sinexd 11.5G local compiles from the dead cachix contract sinnix-iln). All root causes fixed and verified; successor work tracked in sinnix-3gb (kill-policy week eval), sinnix-aqd (polylogued), sinnix-55d (PID1 re-measure), sinnix-878 (kitty leak).

title: pam_lastlog2 SQLite lock contention refuses PAM sessions under agent session storms
description: Chronic (3-9 events/day, every day this week; 8 today): pam_lastlog2(login:session) fails with 'SQL error: database is locked' on /var/lib/lastlog/lastlog2.db when many sessions open concurrently (systemd-stdio-bridge bursts from agent/systemd-run traffic). Impact today 10:44: three run-pXXX.service bridge sessions failed PAM setup entirely ('Failed at step PAM' / 'Operation not permitted') — i.e. this is no longer just the wear-canary role recorded in lynchpin observability_status; it refuses real sessions. Root cause shape: lastlog2's SQLite has no busy_timeout in the PAM path, so concurrent session-open storms hit SQLITE_BUSY instantly. Candidate fixes: (a) drop pam_lastlog2 from the session stack for non-interactive session classes (systemd-run/stdio-bridge) via security.pam.services overrides; (b) drop lastlog2 entirely if the login-history value is nil on this single-operator host (check lynchpin canary dependency first — it deliberately watches lastlog2 for root-SSD wear); (c) upstream busy_timeout. Decide (a) vs (b) with operator; (b) requires re-homing the wear canary.

title: Realm filesystem taxonomy: one pattern per data class instead of three
description:
Operator request 2026-07-10: the layout grew organically and is confusing — organize for clarity, not just performance. Current /realm root after tonight: project/ data/ db/ inbox/ tmp/ cache/ backup/ sinex/ swap/ (+ .Trash). The confusions, concretely:
1. SERVICE STATE has three patterns: /realm/db/{polylogue,machine-telemetry,journal} (nested subvols), /realm/sinex (nested subvol at root), /var/lib/nats (on /persist/MX500). And 'db' is a lie for some residents: journal and sinex blobs are not databases.
2. NAMING: /realm/backup is STAGING for backups whose TARGETS live on /outer-realm/backup — same word, opposite roles.
3. /realm/tmp mixes three lifecycles: worktrees (days, precious-ish), shell TMPDIR (7d-aged), throwaway analysis (session).
4. Capture-shaped data lives outside the lake: raw agent session JSONLs under /persist home dotdirs (.codex/sessions 22G, .config/claude/projects 16G) while their derived archive lives in the lake.

PROPOSED TARGET (decide before executing):
- /realm/state/ replaces db/ and absorbs sinex/ + journal: ALL always-on service state as nested subvols (state/postgres-sinex or state/sinex, state/polylogue, state/machine-telemetry, state/journal, state/nats). One rule: nested subvol => excluded from btrbk => must have its own dump/backup story (assertable! ref sinnix-ow0).
- /realm/cache/ stays (established tonight): regenerable, never backed up.
- /realm/backup/ -> /realm/staging/ (or keep name but document); one rule: plain dir => rides btrbk->borg.
- /realm/tmp/ split: tmp/ (aged throwaway + shell TMPDIR) vs worktrees/ promoted to /realm/worktrees (not aged).
- project/ data/ inbox/ swap/ unchanged; INVENTORY.md gains the one-page taxonomy table (class -> location -> backup story -> lifecycle).
EXECUTION NOTES: each move = mount/path flip with brief service stop (tonight's sinex migration is the template); nats move also frees MX500 writes; do NOT rename during the sinex burn-in window; sinnix-ow0's assertion pattern should encode the invariants (nested-subvol-has-backup-story, no-service-state-on-persist).
acceptance_criteria:
- operator signs off on the taxonomy table before any move
- each data class has exactly one location pattern and a documented backup story in INVENTORY.md
- eval assertions encode: service state only under /realm/state, nested subvols have declared backup coverage
- all moves executed with verified parity + services healthy
notes:
SEMANTIC REFINEMENT (operator, 2026-07-10): /realm/data was conceived as PERSONAL data (self-related: captures, exports, knowledgebase), NOT a media shelf — 'libraries' as a whole may not belong under data/ at all. Proposed split for the sign-off table:
- /realm/data = self-data only: captures/ exports/ knowledgebase/ derived/ secrets/ + deeply-personal library members (finance, photos, genome, private, doc?)
- /realm/media (new root) = consumption/media collections: books, videos, music+audio-features, Steam, web-content, substack, code-archives; model weights arguably cache-class (re-downloadable) or media
- the fuzzy members (doc? edu? photos?) are exactly what the sign-off table must settle — operator taste, not derivable
ALSO IN SCOPE: /outer-realm needs the same one-page treatment — archive/ vs archive-staged/ vs misc/ vs inbox/ are undocumented dumping grounds ('this weird archive and archive-staged... misc/downloads is just a dumping ground'). Proposed semantics: archive/ = deliberate cold append-only storage with provenance; archive-staged/ = triage buffer with an explicit drain policy (either promoted to archive or deleted, never resident); misc/downloads = abolished, contents triaged into inbox flow. Related live fix: .Trash-1000 on /outer-realm restored (9d159eb8) so cleanup is actually possible there again.
META: same-level-mixing rule extends to every root: main nodes only at level 1, per-root INVENTORY/README declaring the node set, lake-lint asserts it.

title: Move DB-backup staging (/persist/backup, 79G) off the MX500 to /realm
description: Impermanence audit 2026-07-10: /persist/backup (machine-telemetry sqlite snapshots, polylogue-sqlite, sinex-postgres dumps) stages 79G of daily backup churn on the worn MX500, which borg then re-drains from the persist snapshot — double-writing every backup byte through the dying disk. Relocate the staging roots (machine-telemetry.nix backupRoot, polylogue-sqlite-backup, sinex pg-dump target) to a /realm location that remains inside the /realm borg job's coverage (NOT under the excluded /realm/cache), preserving off-machine durability while removing MX500 wear. Mind unit RequiresMountsFor + persistence declarations cleanup.
acceptance_criteria:
- all three backup jobs write under /realm and their next scheduled runs succeed
- restored-from-borg test for one artifact passes (coverage preserved)
- /persist/backup removed from disk + declarations; MX500 standing writes drop recorded
notes: Config landed (c2e08d75): all three backupRoots point at /realm/backup, dump service deps realm.mount, tmpfiles owns the root; switch applied. Data migration backup-staging-migrate.service rsyncing /persist/backup -> /realm/backup (79G, idle IO). REMAINING: verify sizes match, delete /persist/backup, confirm next scheduled runs of all three jobs write to the new roots (pg dump fires 03:17 tonight — first dump in 11 days after the masked-timer fix).

title: Relocate Stashbox state off persistent root storage
description: Move Stashbox application state from persistent root storage to /realm/media/stashbox so regenerable data does not ride the frequent persistence backup pipeline. Keep the XDG path stable through a symlink and exclude regenerable members from backup coverage.
acceptance_criteria:
- Stashbox paths point into /realm and the application works
- the old persistence declaration and source are removed
- regenerable members are excluded from frequent backup coverage
